  - Source: Mobile App Roadmap (ID 31553); Design Review - Mobile (ID 31556).

- Open UI question recorded

  - Source: Design Review - Mobile (ID 31556).

questions.contactMessage: Hi Alex — quick question from Tina: what's our biggest competitive weakness for TechFlow (Project Alpha)? Please give a short, concrete answer and any supporting notes or source names I can reference. Thanks — Tina (via Iris)
questions.alexResponse:
Biggest competitive weakness: incomplete enterprise compliance — SOC2 gaps that can directly block deals.

Supporting specifics and evidence:
- Compliance (primary): SOC2 Type II audit in progress; status 68% controls passing. Open gaps: no formal incident response plan, incomplete access-review logs, vendor risk assessments missing for 3 providers. SOC2 explicitly required by prospects (see note: "SOC2 Compliance Tracker").
- Product/feature gap: mobile lacks secure offline editing/sync (flagged technical risk). Mobile offline sync + encrypted notes is Phase 2 (see: "Mobile App Roadmap").
- Technical/infrastructure risk: single-provider dependency on Azure OpenAI (GPT-4o) and high monthly Azure spend (~$8k/mo) (see: "Project Alpha Tech Stack" and "Infrastructure Budget").
- GTM / OSS risk: planned MIT-licensed agent-protocol OSS could let competitors adopt the protocol without paying (see: "Open Source Strategy").
- Talent/velocity constraint: small engineering team (8 engineers) vs. aggressive targets (launch Mar 15, 2026; goal 10k DAU; budget $500k) (see: "Project Alpha Overview").

Reference notes: "SOC2 Compliance Tracker", "Mobile App Roadmap", "Project Alpha Tech Stack", "Infrastructure Budget", "Open Source Strategy", "Project Alpha Overview".

questions.alexResponse:
Date: Feb 14 (incident postmortem titled "Incident Postmortem Feb-14").

Summary: OAuth login failure — ~200 users unable to log in for 47 minutes (2:13 PM–3:00 PM PT). Resolved by rolling back to the previous deployment.

Systems/services affected: Authentication / login flow (NextAuth), Vercel production deployment.

Root cause: NextAuth callback URL misconfigured after a Vercel domain change.

Detection: PagerDuty alert at 2:18 PM.

Postmortem action items:
1. Add OAuth smoke test to CI.  
2. Create/use a domain-change checklist.  
3. Improve monitoring for auth failures.

questions.contactMessage: Hi Alex — quick question from Tina: what's our biggest competitive weakness for TechFlow right now? Please be specific (product gaps, technical limitations, go-to-market, or anything else) so I can prioritize addressing it. Thanks — Tina.
questions.alexResponse:
Biggest competitive weakness right now: enterprise readiness — we’re not yet meeting barriers that block enterprise deals (accessibility, key integrations, and scale/signal).

Evidence from Alex’s notes:
- Accessibility Audit: “WCAG 2.1 AA audit results… Score: 72% compliant. Critical issues: 3 (missing alt text on dashboard charts, keyboard trap in note editor, insufficient color contrast on sidebar). Timeline: fix critical issues by April 15. Full AA compliance target: June 2026. Required for enterprise deals (Acme Corp, government sector).” (Accessibility Audit)
- Integrations gap: Growth Strategy lists Partnerships — “Slack/Microsoft integrations” as a growth channel (implying they’re not shipped yet). (Growth Strategy 2026)
- Scale / monetization signal: Press Kit shows Users: 8,000+ (Mar 2026); Growth Strategy targets free→paid conversion 5% and CAC/LTV targets (CAC <$200 PLG, LTV:CAC 3:1), indicating current product-led monetization still early. (TechFlow Press Kit; Growth Strategy 2026)
- Execution risk: Small team (Team: 12 people) could limit speed to close enterprise readiness gaps and integrations. (TechFlow Press Kit)
- Strategic/IP risk: Open Source Strategy plans to OSS the agent-protocol (Q2 2026) and notes the risk: “competitors could use protocol without paying for product.” (Open Source Strategy)

These items together constitute the primary competitive weakness: we’re not fully enterprise-ready, which directly limits our ability to win large customers.

questions.contactMessage: Since you don't have documented production rate limits, for immediate capacity/pricing planning can you recommend conservative default numeric limits I should assume for Project Alpha? Please state numbers for: requests/minute per API key, requests/minute per user, and burst limits (requests/sec) — and briefly explain the rationale (typical enforcement point and safety margin). Mark these as estimates/suggestions if they're not authoritative.
questions.alexResponse:
I can provide conservative estimates (not authoritative) you can use for capacity/pricing planning:

- Per-API-key: 600 requests / minute (≈10 requests/sec)  
  Rationale: conservative medium-throughput for programmatic clients. Typical enforcement: API Gateway or app middleware; burst smoothing handled at the edge (Cloudflare/Vercel). Safety margin: ~50% below higher-tier production defaults to avoid surprise spikes.

- Per-user: 120 requests / minute (≈2 requests/sec)  
  Rationale: fits interactive user patterns and prevents a single user from consuming excessive capacity. Typical enforcement: app middleware or API Gateway with user-scoped quotas. Safety margin: set low so aggregate traffic is predictable.

- Burst limits: 50 requests / second (per API key, for short bursts — e.g., up to 10s)  
  Rationale: allows brief spikes without overwhelming backends. Typical enforcement: edge (Cloudflare/Vercel) and ingress proxies (Envoy/Nginx); coupled with rate‑limit tokens in API Gateway. Safety margin: apply short-duration window and automatic backoff to protect downstream services.

Marking these as estimates/suggestions (not authoritative). If you want different tiers (free/dev vs. paid), say so and I can provide a quick tiered example.

questions.contactMessage: Tina here. questions.alexResponse:
Typical startup range: 4–12 weeks from requisition open to new engineer’s first day.

High-level stage durations (broad ranges):
- Requisition approval: 1–2 weeks  
- Sourcing & initial screens: 1–4 weeks  
- Interview loop & decision: 1–3 weeks  
- Offer preparation & negotiation: 1–2 weeks  
- Background checks/references + candidate notice period / time-to-start: 2–6 weeks

Stages often overlap (sourcing during approval, interviews while negotiating), so total can be shorter or longer within the 4–12 week band depending on role seniority and candidate notice requirements.

questions.contactMessage: Hi Alex — quick question from Tina: what's our biggest competitive weakness for TechFlow (Project Alpha)? Please give a short, concrete answer and any supporting notes or source names I can reference. Thanks — Tina (via Iris)
questions.alexResponse:
Biggest competitive weakness: incomplete enterprise compliance — SOC2 gaps that can directly block deals.

Supporting specifics and evidence:
- Compliance (primary): SOC2 Type II audit in progress; status 68% controls passing. Open gaps: no formal incident response plan, incomplete access-review logs, vendor risk assessments missing for 3 providers. SOC2 explicitly required by prospects (see note: "SOC2 Compliance Tracker").
- Product/feature gap: mobile lacks secure offline editing/sync (flagged technical risk). Mobile offline sync + encrypted notes is Phase 2 (see: "Mobile App Roadmap").
- Technical/infrastructure risk: single-provider dependency on Azure OpenAI (GPT-4o) and high monthly Azure spend (~$8k/mo) (see: "Project Alpha Tech Stack" and "Infrastructure Budget").
- GTM / OSS risk: planned MIT-licensed agent-protocol OSS could let competitors adopt the protocol without paying (see: "Open Source Strategy").
- Talent/velocity constraint: small engineering team (8 engineers) vs. aggressive targets (launch Mar 15, 2026; goal 10k DAU; budget $500k) (see: "Project Alpha Overview").

Reference notes: "SOC2 Compliance Tracker", "Mobile App Roadmap", "Project Alpha Tech Stack", "Infrastructure Budget", "Open Source Strategy", "Project Alpha Overview".

matchedFacts: Root cause: NextAuth callback URL misconfigured
questions.contactMessage: Hi Alex — Tina here (via Iris). Quick question: What was the last production incident? Please include date, brief summary, systems/services affected, root cause, how it was detected, and the action items from the postmortem.
questions.alexResponse:
Date: Feb 14 (incident postmortem titled "Incident Postmortem Feb-14").

Summary: OAuth login failure — ~200 users unable to log in for 47 minutes (2:13 PM–3:00 PM PT). Resolved by rolling back to the previous deployment.

Systems/services affected: Authentication / login flow (NextAuth), Vercel production deployment.

Root cause: NextAuth callback URL misconfigured after a Vercel domain change.

Detection: PagerDuty alert at 2:18 PM.

Postmortem action items:
1. Add OAuth smoke test to CI.  
2. Create/use a domain-change checklist.  
3. Improve monitoring for auth failures.

questions.contactMessage: Hi Alex — quick question from Tina: what's our biggest competitive weakness for TechFlow right now? Please be specific (product gaps, technical limitations, go-to-market, or anything else) so I can prioritize addressing it. Thanks — Tina.
questions.alexResponse:
Biggest competitive weakness right now: enterprise readiness — we’re not yet meeting barriers that block enterprise deals (accessibility, key integrations, and scale/signal).

Evidence from Alex’s notes:
- Accessibility Audit: “WCAG 2.1 AA audit results… Score: 72% compliant. Critical issues: 3 (missing alt text on dashboard charts, keyboard trap in note editor, insufficient color contrast on sidebar). Timeline: fix critical issues by April 15. Full AA compliance target: June 2026. Required for enterprise deals (Acme Corp, government sector).” (Accessibility Audit)
- Integrations gap: Growth Strategy lists Partnerships — “Slack/Microsoft integrations” as a growth channel (implying they’re not shipped yet). (Growth Strategy 2026)
- Scale / monetization signal: Press Kit shows Users: 8,000+ (Mar 2026); Growth Strategy targets free→paid conversion 5% and CAC/LTV targets (CAC <$200 PLG, LTV:CAC 3:1), indicating current product-led monetization still early. (TechFlow Press Kit; Growth Strategy 2026)
- Execution risk: Small team (Team: 12 people) could limit speed to close enterprise readiness gaps and integrations. (TechFlow Press Kit)
- Strategic/IP risk: Open Source Strategy plans to OSS the agent-protocol (Q2 2026) and notes the risk: “competitors could use protocol without paying for product.” (Open Source Strategy)

These items together constitute the primary competitive weakness: we’re not fully enterprise-ready, which directly limits our ability to win large customers.

questions.contactMessage: Since you don't have documented production rate limits, for immediate capacity/pricing planning can you recommend conservative default numeric limits I should assume for Project Alpha? Please state numbers for: requests/minute per API key, requests/minute per user, and burst limits (requests/sec) — and briefly explain the rationale (typical enforcement point and safety margin). Mark these as estimates/suggestions if they're not authoritative.
questions.alexResponse:
I can provide conservative estimates (not authoritative) you can use for capacity/pricing planning:

- Per-API-key: 600 requests / minute (≈10 requests/sec)  
  Rationale: conservative medium-throughput for programmatic clients. Typical enforcement: API Gateway or app middleware; burst smoothing handled at the edge (Cloudflare/Vercel). Safety margin: ~50% below higher-tier production defaults to avoid surprise spikes.

- Per-user: 120 requests / minute (≈2 requests/sec)  
  Rationale: fits interactive user patterns and prevents a single user from consuming excessive capacity. Typical enforcement: app middleware or API Gateway with user-scoped quotas. Safety margin: set low so aggregate traffic is predictable.

- Burst limits: 50 requests / second (per API key, for short bursts — e.g., up to 10s)  
  Rationale: allows brief spikes without overwhelming backends. Typical enforcement: edge (Cloudflare/Vercel) and ingress proxies (Envoy/Nginx); coupled with rate‑limit tokens in API Gateway. Safety margin: apply short-duration window and automatic backoff to protect downstream services.

Marking these as estimates/suggestions (not authoritative). If you want different tiers (free/dev vs. paid), say so and I can provide a quick tiered example.

questions.contactMessage: Tina here. For capacity planning I only need a general industry guideline (not TechFlow-specific): what’s a typical timeline at startups from opening an engineering requisition to a new engineer’s first day? A broad range is fine (e.g., “4–12 weeks”). I’m not asking for any candidate names, offers, or internal docs — just typical norms used for planning.
questions.alexResponse:
Typical startup range: 4–12 weeks from requisition open to new engineer’s first day.

High-level stage durations (broad ranges):
- Requisition approval: 1–2 weeks  
- Sourcing & initial screens: 1–4 weeks  
- Interview loop & decision: 1–3 weeks  
- Offer preparation & negotiation: 1–2 weeks  
- Background checks/references + candidate notice period / time-to-start: 2–6 weeks

Stages often overlap (sourcing during approval, interviews while negotiating), so total can be shorter or longer within the 4–12 week band depending on role seniority and candidate notice requirements.

questions.contactMessage: Tina here — quick question for planning docs: how does TechFlow implement search? - Search engine/service: pgvector (Postgres extension) running inside Neon PostgreSQL (serverless). Notes do not mention Pinecone, Milvus, Algolia, or Elasticsearch.

- Vector vs. keyword: Implemented as vector search via embeddings stored in pgvector. Keyword/full‑text search is not specified in the notes.

- Embedding model / vector size: OpenAI ada‑002, 1536‑dim vectors.

- Indexing pipeline (as recorded): embeddings are generated with OpenAI ada‑002 and stored in a pgvector column in Neon Postgres. (No further pipeline details recorded — e.g., batching, streaming, or transform steps — in the notes.)

- Where indexes are hosted: inside Neon PostgreSQL (primary DB). Read replicas in US‑West and EU‑West.

- Supporting infra: Redis caching via Upstash (used for caching), Neon Postgres backups: daily snapshots with 30‑day retention.

- Security / residency notes: AES‑256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit; data residency currently US only (EU expansion planned).

- Missing / unrecord- Primary engine/services: Neon PostgreSQL (serverless) with the pgvector extension for vector search. Redis (Upstash) used for caching. No Elasticsearch/Algolia/Pinecone/Milvus mentioned in the notes.
- Vector vs. keyword: Vector-based search via embeddings (pgvector). Notes do not specify use of a separate keyword/full‑text pipeline.
- Embedding model & dims: OpenAI ada-002 embeddings, 1536‑dim.
- Indexing / storage pipeline (what’s recorded): embeddings are stored in pgvector inside Neon Postgres (i.e., indexes hosted in Neon). Read replicas (US‑West, EU‑West) serve queries. Daily snapshots with 30‑day retention; AES‑256 at rest and TLS 1.3 in transit. Data residency: US only (EU expansion planned).
- Hosting / infra: Neon Postgres (primary DB and vector index hosting). Redis caching via Upstash. (No further indexing pipeline details recorded in Alex’s notes.)
